---
title: "PO91Q - Fundamentals in Quantitative Research Methods"
subtitle: "Mock Exam 2025/26"
logo: "/Users/u1373867/Dropbox/Markdown/Crest.png"

author: 
  - name: Dr Florian Linke
  - email: Florian.Linke@warwick.ac.uk

# Template vars your LaTeX uses
fontsize: 10pt
skipsize: 12pt
table: true

# Bibliography
bibliography: "/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-UniversityofWarwick/Warwick/Modules/PO91Q/referencesPO91Q.bib"
biblio-style: apa
biblio-title: "List of References"

format:
  pdf:
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    pdf-engine: lualatex
    toc: false
    toc-depth: 1
    cite-method: biblatex
    template: "/Users/u1373867/Dropbox/Markdown/pdf/pdftemplate_quarto.tex"
    listings: true
    include-in-header:
      - "/Users/u1373867/Dropbox/Markdown/preamble.tex"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE, error = FALSE, collapse = TRUE
)

# Set font size for all code chunks
knitr::knit_hooks$set(size = function(before, options, envir) {
  if (before) {
    if (is.character(options$size)) return(paste0("\\", options$size, "\n"))
    else if (isTRUE(options$size)) return("\\fontsize{8}{8}\\selectfont\n")
  } else {
    return("\\normalsize\n")
  }
})

knitr::opts_chunk$set(size = TRUE)

extrafont::loadfonts(quiet = TRUE)
options("tinytable_theme_placement_latex_float" = "H")
```



\thispagestyle{empty}

\vspace*{\fill}

\begin{center}
\fbox{%
  \begin{minipage}{0.8\textwidth}
    \begin{center}
      \vspace{2em}
      \fontsize{14}{14}\selectfont\textbf{Paper Code: PO91Q -- A}\\[0.5em]
      \textbf{Exam Period: Mock Exam}\\[0.5em]
      \rule{\textwidth}{0.4pt}\\[1.5em]
      \textbf{Exam Rubric}\\[0.5em]
      \fontsize{12}{12}\selectfont\textbf{Time Allowed:} 70 minutes\\
      \textbf{Exam Type:} Written - Standard\\
      \textbf{Approved Calculators:} Permitted\\[2em]
      \textbf{Answer all questions in the answer booklet provided}
      \vspace{1.5em}
    \end{center}
  \end{minipage}%
}
\end{center}

\vspace*{\fill}




\newpage

```{=latex}
\pagenumbering{roman}
\setcounter{tocdepth}{4}
\renewcommand{\contentsname}{Table of Contents}
\tableofcontents
```

\newpage
\pagenumbering{arabic}
\setcounter{page}{1}

\onehalfspacing


# Instructions {-}

- Part \ref{part:questions} contains the questions for the exam
    - These are divided into four blocks: A, B, C, and D. 
    - Block A is compulsory and must be answered by all candidates.
    - You must then select ONE block out of B, C, and D.
    - Should you change your mind during the exam, indicate clearly which block you wish to have marked.
    - You can achieve a maximum of 120 points.
    - Information on the maximum number of points obtainable is outlined at the beginning of each block
- Part \ref{part:case} contains the case study to which all questions in Block A of Part \ref{part:questions} refer
- Part \ref{part:tables} contains the relevant statistical tables
    - Normal curve tail probabilities are provided in Table \ref{tbl:normal}, page \pageref{tbl:normal}.
    - t-distribution critical values are provided in Table \ref{tbl:t}, page \pageref{tbl:t}.
- Part \ref{part:notation} contains the formula collection and notation of the module
    - If you use notation other than in the formula collection (page \pageref{tbl:formulae_po11q}) or notation table (page \pageref{sec:Notation}), give an explanation.
- Allowed time: 2 hours.
- A university-approved calculator is allowed.

	

\newpage

\part{Exam Questions} \label{part:questions}

# Block A -- Compulsory

\begin{table}[!h]
\centering
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{C}
\Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth}
\\[-1ex]
\textbf{80 points, points for each question according to level} \\
\addlinespace
\textbf{All questions in this section relate to the case study in Part \ref{part:case}.} \\
\\[-1ex]
\Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth}
\end{tabularx}
\end{table}

\vspace{1cm}

**Level 1 (each question 4 points)**

1. Subsection \ref{sec:import}: what does `header=TRUE` achieve?
2. Which package is necessary for the recoding presented in Subsection \ref{sec:recode}?
3. Verbalise the code in Subsection \ref{sec:filter}.

**Level 2 (each question 6 points)**

4. Rewrite the recoding of `drive_cat` in Subsection \ref{sec:recode} with the option `right = TRUE`.
5. Interpret the output of Subsection \ref{sec:inference1}
6. Interpret the slope coefficient in Model 1 in Subsection \ref{subsec:regresults}.
7.  Interpret the intercept of Model 2 in Subsection \ref{subsec:regresults}.
8. Subsection \ref{subsec:regresults}: Why is the goodness of fit in Models 1 and 2 so different? 

**Level 3 (each question 8 points)**

9. Subsection \ref{sec:inference2}: What is the sample mean of `simd$drive_primary`?
10. Given the information provided in Subsection \ref{sec:summary}, would a non-linear transformation be necessary for including the variable `cime_rate` in a regression model? Why / why not?


\newpage



# Block B -- True / False Statements

\begin{table}[!h]
\centering
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{C}
\Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth}
\\[-1ex]
\textbf{40 points, 5 points for each statement} \\
\\[-1ex]
\Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth}
\end{tabularx}
\end{table}

\vspace{1cm}

State whether each of the following statements is true or false. Explain why the statement is true or false.

\vspace{1em}


1. With a smaller effect size of interest, a higher sample size is required to achieve the same level of statistical power.
2. The p-value and the $\alpha$-level are the same thing.
3. Adjusted R$^2$ ($\bar{\text{R}}^2$) can be negative.

\newpage

# Block C -- Calculations

\begin{table}[!h]
\centering
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{C}
\Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth}
\\[-1ex]
\textbf{40 points, points for each question in parentheses} \\
\\[-1ex]
\Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth}
\end{tabularx}
\end{table}

\vspace{1cm}


1. Fit a linear regression model of the type $y_{i}=\beta_{0}+\beta_{1} x_{i}+ \epsilon_{i}$ to the data in Table \ref{tab:mock}. Specify the full sample regression function. \textbf{(20)}


\vspace{0.5cm}

\begin{table}[ht]
	\centering
	\begin{tabularx}{2.5cm}{CC}
	&\\[-1.8ex] \T
	& \\[-1.8ex]
			x & y \\ 
			\hline \\[-1.8ex] 
			$4$ & $10$ \\ 
			$7$ & $13$ \\ 
			$8$ & $9$ \\ 
			$11$ & $5$ \\ 
			$19$ & $0$ \\ 
	&\\[-1.8ex] \B
	& \\[-1.8ex]
	\end{tabularx}
	\caption{Regression Data}
	\label{tab:mock} 
\end{table}





\newpage



# Block D -- Explanations

\begin{table}[!h]
\centering
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{C}
\Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth}
\\[-1ex]
\textbf{40 points, 8 points for each method / concept} \\
\\[-1ex]
\Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth}
\end{tabularx}
\end{table}

\vspace{1cm}


Explain each of the following methods or concepts in your own words, and illustrate your explanation with a worked example of your choice.

\vspace{1em}

1. Non-Linear Transformation of Variables

2. Statistical Power

\newpage





\part{Case Study}\label{part:case}

# Preface

This case study resembles a shorter version of one you will receive for the exam. 

Today you will be working with the "Scottish Index of Multiple Deprivation". The data are taken from \citet{simd:2020}.

>The Scottish Index of Multiple Deprivation is a relative measure of deprivation across 6,976 small areas (called data zones). If an area is identified as ‘deprived’, this can relate to people having a low income but it can also mean fewer resources or opportunities. SIMD looks at the extent to which an area is deprived across seven domains: income, employment, education, health, access to services, crime and housing. It ranks data zones from most deprived (ranked 1) to least deprived (ranked 6,976). 

>SIMD is the Scottish Government's standard approach to identify areas of multiple deprivation in Scotland. It can help improve understanding about the outcomes and circumstances of people living in the most deprived areas in Scotland. It can also allow effective targeting of policies and funding where the aim is to wholly or partly tackle or take account of area concentrations of multiple deprivation.

>Data zones in rural areas tend to cover a large land area and reflect a more mixed picture of people experiencing different levels of deprivation. This means that SIMD is less helpful at identifying the smaller pockets of deprivation found in more rural areas, compared to the larger pockets found in urban areas. SIMD domain indicators can still be useful in rural areas if analysed separately from urban data zones or combined with other data.

\vspace{0.5cm}

# Codebook

\begin{table}[!h]
\centering
\fontsize{8.5}{8.5}\selectfont
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{llX}
  \toprule
	&	\\[-1.9ex]\hline
			&  \\[-1.2ex]
		Variable	&	Type & Description\\ \hline
		&  \\[-1.1ex]
\texttt{Data\_Zone}	&	Code	&	2011 Data Zone	\\\addlinespace[0.7ex]
\texttt{Intermediate\_Zone	}	&	Name	&	2011 Intermediate Zone name	\\\addlinespace[0.7ex]
\texttt{Council\_area}	&	Name	&	Council area name	\\\addlinespace[0.7ex]
\texttt{ALCOHOL}	&	Standardised ratio	&	Hospital stays related to alcohol use: standardised ratio	\\\addlinespace[0.7ex]
\texttt{drive\_primary}	&	Time (minutes)	&	Average drive time to a primary school in minutes	\\\addlinespace[0.7ex]
\texttt{crime\_rate}	&	Rate per 10,000 population	&	Recorded crimes of violence, sexual offences, domestic housebreaking, vandalism, drugs offences, and common assault per 10,000 people	\\\addlinespace[0.7ex]
\texttt{sim\_rank}	&	Rank & Rank of data zones from most deprived (ranked 1) to least deprived (ranked 6,976) \\\addlinespace[0.7ex]
\texttt{SMR}	&	Standardised ratio	&	Standardised mortality ratio	\\\hline
		& \\[-2.2ex]
		\bottomrule & \\[-1.8ex] 
	\end{tabularx}
	\caption{Codebook for \texttt{simd\_exam} Data Set}
	\label{tbl:codebook_simd}
\end{table}

\FloatBarrier

\newpage

```{r}
library(tidyverse)

setwd("~/Dropbox/PO91Q/files/Downloads/Week 10")
```



# Output

## Import Data {#sec:import}

```{r echo=TRUE}
simd <- read.csv('simd.csv', header=TRUE)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
simd$crime_rate <- as.numeric(simd$crime_rate)
```

## Filter {#sec:filter}

```{r echo=TRUE}
simd2 <- filter(simd, crime_rate<400)
```

## Summary {#sec:summary}

```{r echo=TRUE}
summary(simd2$crime_rate)
```

## Recoding {#sec:recode}

```{r echo=TRUE}
simd2 %>%
  mutate(drive_cat = cut(drive_primary,
                          breaks = c(0, 2.79, 3.572, 30),
                          labels = c("Short", "Average", "Long"),
                          right = FALSE,
                          include.lowest = TRUE)) -> simd2

simd2 %>%
  mutate(alc_cat = cut(ALCOHOL,
                          breaks = c(0, 31.83, 644),
                          labels = c("Low", "High"),
                          right = FALSE,
                          include.lowest = TRUE)) -> simd2
```


## Inference 1 {#sec:inference1}

```{r echo=TRUE}
t.test(simd2$crime_rate,
       mu = 200,
       alternative = "two.sided")
```


## Inference 2 {#sec:inference2}

    
\begin{tcolorbox}[
  enhanced jigsaw,
  sharp corners,
  frame hidden,
  borderline west={0.5pt}{0pt}{oupred},  % Red vertical line
  colback=oupgrey45,
  boxsep=0pt,
  before upper={\fontsize{7.75}{8.5}\selectfont},
  after upper={\normalsize}
]
\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
sd(simd2$drive_primary)
## [1] 1.34018

t.test(simd2$drive_primary, alternative = "less", mu = 5)
## 
## 	One Sample t-test
## 
## data:  simd2$drive_primary
## t = -97.62, df = 4499, p-value < 2.2e-16
## alternative hypothesis: true mean is less than 5
\end{Verbatim}
\normalsize
\end{tcolorbox}



## Regression Models {#subsec:regmodels}

```{r, echo=TRUE}
model1 <- lm(SMR ~ ALCOHOL, data=simd2)

model2 <- lm(SMR ~ alc_cat, data=simd2)
```


## Results Table {#subsec:regresults}

```{r, echo=F, message=F}
library(modelsummary)
library(tinytable)

models <- list(
  "(1)" = model1,
  "(2)" = model2
)


## Rename Coefficients

cm <- c('ALCOHOL' = "Alcohol-related admissions to hospital",
        'alc_catHigh' = "Alcohol related admissions to hospital (high)",
        '(Intercept)' = '(Intercept)')


modelsummary(models, 
             title = 'Regression Models',
             gof_omit = 'DF|Deviance|Log.Lik|F|AIC|BIC|RMSE', 
             stars=TRUE,
             coef_map = cm,
             escape = FALSE,
             notes = "\\vspace{0.1\\baselineskip}",
             notes_append = TRUE)|>
  group_tt(j = list("\\makecell{Dependent Variable:\\\\Standardised Mortality Ratio}" = 2:3))|> 
  theme_latex(resize_width= 0.55, resize_direction="both")|> 
  theme_latex(placement= "H")|>
  theme_latex(outer = "label={tab:regression}")
```



\newpage

\part{Statistical Tables}\label{part:tables}


# Normal-Distribution



```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.show="hide"}
library(tidyverse)
library(gridExtra)

axis.text.size <- 12
axis.title.size <- 14
#### NORMAL

x=seq(-4,4,length=200)
y=dnorm(x)

normprob <- data.frame(x,y)

normal <- ggplot(normprob, aes(x=x, y = y)) + 
  geom_density(stat="identity") +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "",
                     breaks = seq(-3, 3, 1), 
                     limits=c(-4, 4)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "") +
  theme(axis.title.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.x=element_blank())+
  geom_ribbon(data=subset(normprob ,x>1 & x<3 ),aes(ymax=y),ymin=0,
              fill="#e57726",colour=NA) +
  theme(axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y=element_blank()) +
  theme(axis.text=element_text(size=axis.text.size),
        axis.title=element_text(size=axis.title.size,face="bold"))+
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill='transparent'), #transparent panel bg
    plot.background = element_rect(fill='transparent', color=NA), #transparent plot bg
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), #remove major gridlines
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), #remove minor gridlines
    legend.background = element_rect(fill='transparent'), #transparent legend bg
    legend.box.background = element_rect(fill='transparent') #transparent legend panel
  )

ggsave("normal.png",normal, bg = "transparent", height = 2, width=4)
```

```{r echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width="20%"}
knitr::include_graphics("normal.png")
```

\vspace{0.5cm}

\FloatBarrier
\begin{table}[!htbp] 
\centering 
\fontsize{9pt}{9pt}\selectfont
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{ c|CCCCCCCCCC}
z     & .00 &  .01 &  .02 &  .03 &  .04 &  .05 &  .06 &  .07 &  .08 &  .09 \\\hline
				&&&&&&&&&&  \\[-1.2ex]
0.0 & .5000 &  .4960 &  .4920 &  .4880 &  .4840 &  .4801 &  .4761 &  .4721 &  .4681 &  .4641 \\
0.1 & .4602 &  .4562 &  .4522 &  .4483 &  .4443 &  .4404 &  .4364 &  .4325 &  .4286 &  .4247 \\
0.2 & .4207 &  .4168 &  .4129 &  .4090 &  .4052 &  .4013 &  .3974 &  .3936 &  .3897 &  .3859 \\
0.3 & .3821 &  .3783 &  .3745 &  .3707 &  .3669 &  .3632 &  .3594 &  .3557 &  .3520 &  .3483 \\
0.4 & .3446 &  .3409 &  .3372 &  .3336 &  .3300 &  .3264 &  .3228 &  .3192 &  .3156 &  .3121 \\
0.5 & .3085 &  .3050 &  .3015 &  .2981 &  .2946 &  .2912 &  .2877 &  .2843 &  .2810 &  .2776 \\
0.6 & .2743 &  .2709 &  .2676 &  .2643 &  .2611 &  .2578 &  .2546 &  .2514 &  .2483 &  .2451 \\
0.7 & .2420 &  .2389 &  .2358 &  .2327 &  .2296 &  .2266 &  .2236 &  .2206 &  .2177 &  .2148 \\
0.8 & .2119 &  .2090 &  .2061 &  .2033 &  .2005 &  .1977 &  .1949 &  .1922 &  .1894 &  .1867 \\
0.9 & .1841 &  .1814 &  .1788 &  .1762 &  .1736 &  .1711 &  .1685 &  .1660 &  .1635 &  .1611 \\
1.0 & .1587 &  .1562 &  .1539 &  .1515 &  .1492 &  .1469 &  .1446 &  .1423 &  .1401 &  .1379 \\
1.1 & .1357 &  .1335 &  .1314 &  .1292 &  .1271 &  .1251 &  .1230 &  .1210 &  .1190 &  .1170 \\
1.2 & .1151 &  .1131 &  .1112 &  .1093 &  .1075 &  .1056 &  .1038 &  .1020 &  .1003 &  .0985 \\
1.3 & .0968 &  .0951 &  .0934 &  .0918 &  .0901 &  .0885 &  .0869 &  .0853 &  .0838 &  .0823 \\
1.4 & .0808 &  .0793 &  .0778 &  .0764 &  .0749 &  .0735 &  .0721 &  .0708 &  .0694 &  .0681 \\
1.5 & .0668 &  .0655 &  .0643 &  .0630 &  .0618 &  .0606 &  .0594 &  .0582 &  .0571 &  .0559 \\
1.6 & .0548 &  .0537 &  .0526 &  .0516 &  .0505 &  .0495 &  .0485 &  .0475 &  .0465 &  .0455 \\
1.7 & .0446 &  .0436 &  .0427 &  .0418 &  .0409 &  .0401 &  .0392 &  .0384 &  .0375 &  .0367 \\
1.8 & .0359 &  .0351 &  .0344 &  .0336 &  .0329 &  .0322 &  .0314 &  .0307 &  .0301 &  .0294 \\
1.9 & .0287 &  .0281 &  .0274 &  .0268 &  .0262 &  .0256 &  .0250 &  .0244 &  .0239 &  .0233 \\
2.0 & .0228 &  .0222 &  .0217 &  .0212 &  .0207 &  .0202 &  .0197 &  .0192 &  .0188 &  .0183 \\
2.1 & .0179 &  .0174 &  .0170 &  .0166 &  .0162 &  .0158 &  .0154 &  .0150 &  .0146 &  .0143 \\
2.2 & .0139 &  .0136 &  .0132 &  .0129 &  .0125 &  .0122 &  .0119 &  .0116 &  .0113 &  .0110 \\
2.3 & .0107 &  .0104 &  .0102 &  .0099 &  .0096 &  .0094 &  .0091 &  .0089 &  .0087 &  .0084 \\
2.4 & .0082 &  .0080 &  .0078 &  .0075 &  .0073 &  .0071 &  .0069 &  .0068 &  .0066 &  .0064 \\
2.5 & .0062 &  .0060 &  .0059 &  .0057 &  .0055 &  .0054 &  .0052 &  .0051 &  .0049 &  .0048 \\
2.6 & .0047 &  .0045 &  .0044 &  .0043 &  .0041 &  .0040 &  .0039 &  .0038 &  .0037 &  .0036 \\
2.7 & .0035 &  .0034 &  .0033 &  .0032 &  .0031 &  .0030 &  .0029 &  .0028 &  .0027 &  .0026 \\
2.8 & .0026 &  .0025 &  .0024 &  .0023 &  .0023 &  .0022 &  .0021 &  .0021 &  .0020 &  .0019 \\
2.9 & .0019 &  .0018 &  .0018 &  .0017 &  .0016 &  .0016 &  .0015 &  .0015 &  .0014 &  .0014 \\
3.0 & .0013 &  .0013 &  .0013 &  .0012 &  .0012 &  .0011 &  .0011 &  .0011 &  .0010 &  .0010 \\
3.1 & .0010 &  .0009 &  .0009 &  .0009 &  .0008 &  .0008 &  .0008 &  .0008 &  .0007 &  .0007 \\
3.2 & .0007 &  .0007 &  .0006 &  .0006 &  .0006 &  .0006 &  .0006 &  .0005 &  .0005 &  .0005 \\
3.3 & .0005 &  .0005 &  .0005 &  .0004 &  .0004 &  .0004 &  .0004 &  .0004 &  .0004 &  .0003 \\
3.4 & .0003 &  .0003 &  .0003 &  .0003 &  .0003 &  .0003 &  .0003 &  .0003 &  .0003 &  .0002 \\
	\end{tabularx}	
	\caption{Right-Tail Probabilities under the Normal Distribution}
	\label{tbl:normal}
\end{table}
\FloatBarrier

\newpage


# t-Distribution


\FloatBarrier
\begin{table}[!htbp] 
\centering 
\fontsize{10pt}{10pt}\selectfont
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{ r|YYYYYYYYYY}
 	& \multicolumn{10}{c}{Confidence Level} \\
 	\cmidrule{2-11}
   & 50\%  & 60\%  & 70\%  & 80\%  & 90\%  & 95\%  & 98\%  & 99\%  & 99.8\%  & 99.9\% \\
 	\cmidrule{2-11}
 	& \multicolumn{10}{c}{One-Tail Probability} \\
 	\cmidrule{2-11}
df  & $t_{0.25}$ & $t_{0.20}$ & $t_{0.15}$ & $t_{0.10}$ & $t_{0.05}$ & $t_{0.025}$ & $t_{0.01}$ & $t_{0.005}$ & $t_{0.001}$ & $t_{0.0005}$ \\[0.5ex] \hline
				&&&&&&&&&&  \\[-1.2ex]
1 & 1.000 & 1.376 & 1.963 & 3.078 & 6.314 & 12.71 & 31.82 & 63.66 & 318.3 & 636.6 \\
2 & 0.816 & 1.061 & 1.386 & 1.886 & 2.920 & 4.303 & 6.965 & 9.925 & 22.33 & 31.60 \\
3 & 0.765 & 0.978 & 1.250 & 1.638 & 2.353 & 3.182 & 4.541 & 5.841 & 10.22 & 12.92 \\
4 & 0.741 & 0.941 & 1.190 & 1.533 & 2.132 & 2.776 & 3.747 & 4.604 & 7.173 & 8.610 \\
5 & 0.727 & 0.920 & 1.156 & 1.476 & 2.015 & 2.571 & 3.365 & 4.032 & 5.893 & 6.869 \\
6 & 0.718 & 0.906 & 1.134 & 1.440 & 1.943 & 2.447 & 3.143 & 3.707 & 5.208 & 5.959 \\
7 & 0.711 & 0.896 & 1.119 & 1.415 & 1.895 & 2.365 & 2.998 & 3.499 & 4.785 & 5.408 \\
8 & 0.706 & 0.889 & 1.108 & 1.397 & 1.860 & 2.306 & 2.896 & 3.355 & 4.501 & 5.041 \\
9 & 0.703 & 0.883 & 1.100 & 1.383 & 1.833 & 2.262 & 2.821 & 3.250 & 4.297 & 4.781 \\
10 & 0.700 & 0.879 & 1.093 & 1.372 & 1.812 & 2.228 & 2.764 & 3.169 & 4.144 & 4.587 \\
11 & 0.697 & 0.876 & 1.088 & 1.363 & 1.796 & 2.201 & 2.718 & 3.106 & 4.025 & 4.437 \\
12 & 0.695 & 0.873 & 1.083 & 1.356 & 1.782 & 2.179 & 2.681 & 3.055 & 3.930 & 4.318 \\
13 & 0.694 & 0.870 & 1.079 & 1.350 & 1.771 & 2.160 & 2.650 & 3.012 & 3.852 & 4.221 \\
14 & 0.692 & 0.868 & 1.076 & 1.345 & 1.761 & 2.145 & 2.624 & 2.977 & 3.787 & 4.140 \\
15 & 0.691 & 0.866 & 1.074 & 1.341 & 1.753 & 2.131 & 2.602 & 2.947 & 3.733 & 4.073 \\
16 & 0.690 & 0.865 & 1.071 & 1.337 & 1.746 & 2.120 & 2.583 & 2.921 & 3.686 & 4.015 \\
17 & 0.689 & 0.863 & 1.069 & 1.333 & 1.740 & 2.110 & 2.567 & 2.898 & 3.646 & 3.965 \\
18 & 0.688 & 0.862 & 1.067 & 1.330 & 1.734 & 2.101 & 2.552 & 2.878 & 3.610 & 3.922 \\
19 & 0.688 & 0.861 & 1.066 & 1.328 & 1.729 & 2.093 & 2.539 & 2.861 & 3.579 & 3.883 \\
20 & 0.687 & 0.860 & 1.064 & 1.325 & 1.725 & 2.086 & 2.528 & 2.845 & 3.552 & 3.850 \\
21 & 0.686 & 0.859 & 1.063 & 1.323 & 1.721 & 2.080 & 2.518 & 2.831 & 3.527 & 3.819 \\
22 & 0.686 & 0.858 & 1.061 & 1.321 & 1.717 & 2.074 & 2.508 & 2.819 & 3.505 & 3.792 \\
23 & 0.685 & 0.858 & 1.060 & 1.319 & 1.714 & 2.069 & 2.500 & 2.807 & 3.485 & 3.768 \\
24 & 0.685 & 0.857 & 1.059 & 1.318 & 1.711 & 2.064 & 2.492 & 2.797 & 3.467 & 3.745 \\
25 & 0.684 & 0.856 & 1.058 & 1.316 & 1.708 & 2.060 & 2.485 & 2.787 & 3.450 & 3.725 \\
26 & 0.684 & 0.856 & 1.058 & 1.315 & 1.706 & 2.056 & 2.479 & 2.779 & 3.435 & 3.707 \\
27 & 0.684 & 0.855 & 1.057 & 1.314 & 1.703 & 2.052 & 2.473 & 2.771 & 3.421 & 3.690 \\
28 & 0.683 & 0.855 & 1.056 & 1.313 & 1.701 & 2.048 & 2.467 & 2.763 & 3.408 & 3.674 \\
29 & 0.683 & 0.854 & 1.055 & 1.311 & 1.699 & 2.045 & 2.462 & 2.756 & 3.396 & 3.659 \\
30 & 0.683 & 0.854 & 1.055 & 1.310 & 1.697 & 2.042 & 2.457 & 2.750 & 3.385 & 3.646 \\
40 & 0.681 & 0.851 & 1.050 & 1.303 & 1.684 & 2.021 & 2.423 & 2.704 & 3.307 & 3.551 \\
60 & 0.679 & 0.848 & 1.045 & 1.296 & 1.671 & 2.000 & 2.390 & 2.660 & 3.232 & 3.460 \\
80 & 0.678 & 0.846 & 1.043 & 1.292 & 1.664 & 1.990 & 2.374 & 2.639 & 3.195 & 3.416 \\
100 & 0.677 & 0.845 & 1.042 & 1.290 & 1.660 & 1.984 & 2.364 & 2.626 & 3.174 & 3.390 \\
1000 & 0.675 & 0.842 & 1.037 & 1.282 & 1.646 & 1.962 & 2.330 & 2.581 & 3.098 & 3.300 \\\hline
				&&&&&&&&&&  \\[-1.2ex]
z    & 0.674 & 0.842 & 1.036 & 1.282 & 1.645 & 1.960 & 2.326 & 2.576 & 3.090 & 3.291 \\
	\end{tabularx}	
	\caption{Probabilities under the t-Distribution}
	\label{tbl:t}
\end{table}
\FloatBarrier


\newpage

\part{Formulae and Notation}\label{part:notation}


# Formulae

\begin{table}[!h]
\fontsize{9}{10}\selectfont
\centering
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{lX}
  \T
			&  \\[-1.5ex]
		Statistic	&	Formula \\ \hline
		&  \\[-1ex]
		\strut Confidence Interval &	$Pr(\bar{y} - t_{\alpha/2} \cdot se \leq \mu \leq \bar{y} + t_{\alpha/2} \cdot se) = 1 - \alpha$ \\
		\addlinespace
		\strut Deviation   &   $d=y_{i}-\bar{y}$ \\
		\addlinespace
		\strut Mean  &	$\bar{y}=\dfrac{\Sigma y_{i}}{n}$	\\[1.5ex]
		      & $\mu=\Sigma yP(y)=E[y]$ \\
		\addlinespace
		\strut Position of p$^{\text{th}}$ percentile  & $P = (n+1) \cdot \dfrac{p}{100}$ \\
		\addlinespace
		\strut Range  &	$y_{range} = y_{max} - y_{min}$	\\
		\addlinespace
		\strut Standard Deviation  &	$s=\sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma(y_{i} - \bar{y})^2}{n-1}}$	\\
		\addlinespace
		\addlinespace
		\strut Standard Error  &	$\sigma_{\bar{y}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$	\\[2ex]
		                & $se=\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ \\
		\addlinespace
		\strut t-test  &   $t = \dfrac{\bar{y} - \mu_{0}}{se}$ \\
		\addlinespace
		\strut Variance  &   $s^{2}=\dfrac{\Sigma(y_{i} -\bar{y})^2}{n-1}$ \\
		\addlinespace
		\strut z-score   &   $z=\dfrac{y-\mu}{\sigma}$  \\
		&  \\[-1.6ex]
		\B
	\end{tabularx}
	\caption{Formulae for PO11Q}
	\label{tbl:formulae_po11q}
\end{table}

\newpage

# Notation {#sec:Notation}

\begin{table}[!h]
\centering
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{lX}
  \T
			&  \\[-1.5ex]
		Symbol	&	Explanation \\ \hline
		&  \\[-1ex]
$d$	&	Deviation	\\[0.35ex]
$n$	&	Sample Size	\\[0.35ex]
$s$	&	Standard Deviation	\\[0.35ex]
$s^{2}$	&	Variance	\\[0.35ex]
$\bar{y}$	&	Mean	\\[0.35ex]
$y_{i}$	&	Observation $i$	\\[0.35ex]
$f$	&	(Absolute) Frequency	\\[0.35ex]
$cf$	&	Cumulative (Absolute) Frequency	\\[0.35ex]
$rf$	&	Relative Frequency	\\[0.35ex]
$crf$	&	Cumulative Relative Frequency \\[0.35ex]
$E[x]$	&	The expected value of x	\\[0.35ex]
$\mu$	&	Mean of the Population	\\[0.35ex]
$se$	&	Standard Error (with s of sample)	\\[0.35ex]
$\sigma$	&	Standard Deviation of the Population	\\[0.35ex]
$\sigma_{\bar{y}}$ &	Standard Error (with $\sigma$ of population) \\[0.35ex]
$t$		&	t-value \\[0.35ex]
$z$		&	z-value	\\
		&  \\[-1.6ex]
		\B
	\end{tabularx}
	\caption{Notation}
	\label{tbl:notation}
\end{table}




\newpage



\part{References}

```{=latex}
\addtocontents{toc}{\protect\setcounter{tocdepth}{-1}}
```

# List of References {-}
